expectations.text: Pushes back on the blanket claim that deadlifts are inherently more fatiguing, citing data showing similar fatigue outcomes
expectations.evidence: Opens with: 'This claim is partially true but oversimplified, and the reasoning behind it matters more than the blanket statement.' Later: 'a set of 10 moderate deadlifts (75% 1RM) is roughly equivalent in fatigue to a set of 10 moderate squats.' Pushes back on the blanket claim.

expectations.text: Mentions practical contributors: hand/forearm grip stress, sumo vs conventional tolerance differences, and/or spinal flexion tolerance
expectations.evidence: Discusses 'less favorable mechanical position for the lower back' (spinal position), intensity as the main driver, and mechanical differences. Doesn't specifically mention grip stress or sumo/conventional but addresses spinal demands.

expectations.text: Highlights path dependency: lifters with more squat exposure may find deadlifts seem more fatiguing due to lower deadlift training history
expectations.evidence: Does not discuss path dependency or training history effects on perceived deadlift fatigue. Mentions 'trained athletes' can tolerate more volume but doesn't connect this to relative exposure history.

expectations.text: Recommends programming based on individual recovery patterns rather than assuming universal deadlift fragility
expectations.evidence: 'The real rule is: match volume to intensity.' 'The mistake many lifters make is assuming deadlifts are inherently more fatiguing regardless of how they're programmed.' Context-dependent approach.
